items.q: What does the 'tragedy of the commons' describe?
items.correct: Shared resources tend to be overexploited because individuals benefit personally while sharing costs.
items.misconception: It is a play about poor people.
items.why: Students take 'commons' as a place. Hardin's metaphor describes a STRUCTURAL PROBLEM: rational individual choices lead to collective ruin when costs are externalised - applies to overfishing, climate, and groundwater.

items.q: What is the leading driver of global deforestation?
items.correct: Agriculture (especially cattle ranching, soy, palm oil).
items.why: Students think trees are felled for wood. While logging contributes, the largest driver is land conversion for agriculture - food production drives most tropical forest loss, especially commodities like beef and palm oil.

items.q: What process moves carbon from the atmosphere into plants?
items.why: Students confuse the directions. Photosynthesis takes CO2 OUT of the atmosphere (plants build sugars); respiration RELEASES CO2. Both run simultaneously in plants, but only photosynthesis fixes new carbon.

items.q: Which cycle has no significant atmospheric phase?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Phosphorus. “Carbon” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Average time in a reservoir is called?
items.why: It's Residence time. “Flux” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Approximate residence time of CO2 in the atmosphere in years (centennial estimate)?
items.why: The real figure is 100 years. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
